routes.overview: The original first-ascent route via the southwest side of Mount Goode, a serious alpine climb featuring a steep couloir and exposed scrambling terrain.
routes.fa: Wolf Bauer, Philip Dickett, Joe Halwax, Jack Hossack, George MacGowan (July 5, 1936)
routes.approach: Trailhead at Rainy Pass on Highway 20. Hike south on Pacific Crest Trail (PCT) 10 miles, then turn onto North Fork Bridge Creek trail. Trail becomes overgrown after Grizzly Creek crossing; direct route via slabs is preferred. Cross Goode Glacier base and traverse west along glacier, navigating broad steep gully hugging eastern buttress side.
routes.beta: The easiest way up Mount Goode though still Class 4. Route ascends a broad, steep gully with largely poor rock quality. Traverse moraine to reach the Southwest Couloir section, climbing through steep gully with massive loose rocks. 4-day expedition typical; approach/exit each require full days.
routes.descent: Descend via rappel (2-3 rappels) and downclimbing through gully system, reversing the ascent route.
routes.descentText: Rappel twice from summit area, then downclimb through loose gullies, retracing approach. Hazardous in wet conditions due to loose rock and exposure.
knownHazards: Loose rock throughout route
Poor rock quality
Icy/frozen conditions from temperature fluctuations
Exposed ledges with strong winds
Glacier rumbling and instability
routes.bestSeason: Best between late July and early September when snow bridges are established and water access is reliable. Avoid early season due to snow bridge instability and late season due to increased rockfall hazard from warming.
routes.detailedRack: Alpine rock rack including cams, nuts, tricams, and slings. Helmet essential. Crampons and ice axe required for glacier travel.

emergency.notes: Remote location requires helicopter rescue via North Cascades National Park; satellite communication recommended

blurb: Mount Goode is the highest peak (9,200 feet) in North Cascades National Park, Washington. Located in the remote heart of the North Cascades between the Skagit River and Lake Chelan, this alpine peak has no easy summit route and is inaccessible from any road, making it one of the most isolated major peaks in Washington State. The mountain is composed of gneiss with alpine rock terrain and features a dramatic 6,000-foot relief on its north face.
hierarchyNote: CRITICAL DATA DISCREPANCY: The 5 route IDs provided (Gunsight Pass, North Ridge, Southeast Ridge, Direct North Face, West Face) do not match published climbing route names from any primary sources (Mountain Project, SummitPost, Wikipedia, Peakbagger, climbing guidebooks). Primary sources document only 4 established named routes on Mount Goode: (1) Northeast Buttress (5.5, IV+, FA 1966), (2) Southwest Couloir/Southeast Ridge (Class 4, FA 1936), (3) Megalodon Ridge (5.10, IV+, FA 2007), and (4) Southeast Couloir via Park Creek. The provided route names appear to be either local informal names, legacy data, or potentially incorrectly generated data. Only Southeast Ridge could be mapped to a real documented route (Southwest Couloir/Southeast Ridge). All other routes lack corroborating sources in primary climbing databases.

DATA QUALITY ALERT: Four of the five routes provided (Continental Divide, Nisqually Glacier, Summit Pyramid, Crystal Traverse) do not appear in any documented Mount Shuksan route lists from authoritative sources (Mountain Project, SummitPost, Peakbagger, Mountaineers.org, NPS, Alpine Institute, Fred Beckey's Cascade Alpine Guide references, Cascade Climbers.com). Only the North Face route matches documented sources. Nisqually Glacier is confirmed to be on Mount Rainier, not Shuksan. These route IDs require database review/correction. Peak identity (Mount Shuksan, 9131 ft, North Cascades, Whatcom County, WA) is correct.
blurb: Mount Shuksan (9,131 ft) is an iconic glaciated peak in North Cascades National Park, immediately east of Mount Baker in Whatcom County, Washington. Composed of Shuksan greenschist metamorphic rock, the peak is arguably the most photographed mountain in the North Cascades and was first ascended by Asahel Curtis and W. Montelius Price on September 7, 1906. The mountain's name derives from the Lummi word meaning 'high peak' and is surrounded by nine named glaciers.

routes.overview: A classic steep alpine ice and snow route on one of the North Cascades' most dramatic mountain faces, ascending 40-50 degree terrain on the snow- and glacier-clad shoulder dividing the White Salmon and Price Glacier cirques.
routes.bestSeason: Late July through August provides the most stable snow and ice conditions, though the route can be climbed May through September depending on snow conditions and weather.
knownHazards: Large avalanches from the very active Hanging Glacier can sweep the approach area
Crevasses requiring roped glacier travel
Sustained steep ice and snow exposure
Rapid weather changes typical of North Cascades
Route finding complexity in white-out conditions
proTips: Early morning starts take advantage of firm snow conditions before afternoon softening
The route can be done as a long day ascent or with bivy camp overlooking Price Lake
Fast movement and early timing critical for safe conditions on steep terrain
White Salmon Valley approach is notoriously brush-choked; allow extra time for approach
watchOut: Soft afternoon snow can create dangerous uncontrolled sliding on 50+ degree terrain
The exposed White Salmon Valley approach is very dense with alder and requires careful navigation
Crevasse hazard increases as season progresses and snow bridges weaken
routes.approach: From Glacier, WA, obtain backcountry permits at Park Service Ranger Station in Marbelmount, then drive to White Salmon Lodge near Mount Baker Ski Area. The approach follows the brush-choked White Salmon Valley to reach the North Face base around 5,500 feet. Under good snow cover, a scenic traverse descends to White Salmon Glacier; lower snow requires contouring around rock bands and stream crossings. Total approach distance approximately 7-8 miles with significant bushwhacking.
routes.descent: Descent typically follows the White Salmon Glacier with high snowfield traverses to avoid dense vegetation on return. Downclimb or rappel as needed based on snow/ice conditions.
routes.descentText: From the summit, descend via the White Salmon Glacier, which may require downclimbing or rappelling depending on snow and ice conditions. High snowfield traverses allow avoidance of the dense alder and brush in the lower White Salmon Valley. Total descent time typically 4-5 hours to base camp.

timing.recommendedStart: 3:00-4:00 AM from base camp for optimal snow conditions

routes.detailedRack: Standard glacier travel gear: two tools, crampons, ice screws (2-3 pieces), snow pickets, crevasse rescue kit including pulleys and slings. A 60m rope is essential. Late season ascents documented using approximately 6 ice screws and basic rock rack.
routes.proNeeds: Glacier travel skills essential; experience with steep ice climbing to AI2-3 standard; solid crevasse rescue capability required for the team.
whatToBring: Glacier gear (ice axe, crampons, screws, pickets)
Food and stove (fuel needs testing in cold)
routes.comms: Cell service unreliable in the backcountry; satellite communicator recommended for emergency contact.

routes.corrections: Nisqually Glacier is located on Mount Rainier (~90 miles south), not Mount Shuksan. No Nisqually Glacier route exists on Mount Shuksan.

Summit Pyramid is not a standalone route. It is the final 500-600 foot section of multiple Mount Shuksan routes (Sulphide Glacier, Fisher Chimneys, etc.), consisting of Class 3 rock gully or snow climbing to reach the 9,131 ft summit.

routes.fa: Fred Beckey and Clifford Schmidtke, July 5, 1946
knownHazards: Loose, friable greenschist throughout the route
Sparse protection opportunities on rock
Avalanche danger on gully in spring and early season
Occasional rockfall in the main gully
Difficult routefinding in upper terrain
routes.overview: The classic and most frequently climbed route on Nooksack Tower, ascending an 800-foot 50-degree ice couloir on the north face followed by class 4–5 scrambling along the north arête to the summit.
routes.beta: The standard approach uses the Beckey-Schmidtke line up the north face. Start in the right side of the approach gully, work up and left to gain the bergschrund at about 6,500 feet. Front-point up the 50-degree ice/snow couloir for approximately 800 feet. Exit left onto a sloping belay ledge and transition to rock shoes for the final 1,200 feet of technical scrambling and climbing. The route demands careful belaying, solid mountaineering skills, and tolerance for loose rock and poor protection.
routes.approach: Drive Mount Baker Highway (WA 542) east 13 miles from Glacier to the Nooksack River bridge. Turn left immediately before the bridge onto Nooksack River Road (USFS #32). At 0.5 miles, stay left; at 1.4 miles, stay right; park at Ruth Creek washout (2,156 feet) at 2.3 miles. Hike the abandoned road for 2 miles to Nooksack Cirque trailhead (2,550 feet). Ford the Nooksack River and proceed up the forested mountainside east of the creek through thick brush and forest. Gain approximately 1,000 feet to reach Price Lake (4,800 feet), then scramble northeast up the moraine to camp sites around 5,900 feet between Price and East Nooksack glaciers. Approach to the bergschrund adds another 1,500–1,700 feet of glacier travel and steep snow/ice terrain.
routes.descent: Descend via the same north face route using 10–12 rappels from pitons or anchors in the rock and ice. Rappel down the ice couloir in stages to the bergschrund, then traverse and descend back to camp on Price Glacier. The descent typically requires careful anchor placement and team management due to exposed terrain and loose rock.

timing.recommendedStart: Dawn from camp at 5,900 feet; camp approach typically takes 3–5 hours from trailhead parking

routes.bestSeason: July and August provide the most stable weather and reliable climbing conditions. Snow consolidation is generally better mid-summer, reducing avalanche hazard. June can have high water crossings and incomplete snow consolidation; September begins shorter daylight and changing weather patterns. Winter and spring are generally not recommended due to avalanche danger, poor visibility, and insufficient daylight.
routes.detailedRack: Bring a full traditional rack including nuts (0.5–1.5 inches), a selection of cams (0.5–3 inches), slings, multiple carabiners (locking and non-locking), belay device rated for ice, and ice screws (several 60cm and 65cm screws). Rock protection on Nooksack is sparse and marginal; some anchor rely on piton placement. Plan for significant raps down the gully—bring enough rappel anchors or expect to leave gear.
routes.proNeeds: Traditional rock climbing protection is challenging due to poor rock quality and sparse horizontal cracks. Ice screw anchors are more reliable and should be favored. Expect to place pitons in marginal placements for belay and rappel anchors. Pre-placed anchors from previous ascents may exist but cannot be relied upon. Advance planning for rappel anchors is critical.
proTips: Approach via Price Glacier from Price Lake; the route is straightforward once you locate the bergschrund
Time the ascent for dawn to maximize daylight for the descent
Bring a GPS or mark waypoints carefully; routefinding in upper rock terrain can be confusing
The brass Mountaineers register on the summit confirms successful ascents
Expect the climb to take longer than anticipated due to rock quality and routefinding
Consider a three-person or four-person team for efficiency with belays and rappels
Avoid this peak in spring or early summer when the couloir is snow-laden and avalanche risk is high
watchOut: Rockfall from above, especially afternoon sun-loosened rocks—traverse quickly past exposed sections
Poor rock quality means limited protection and difficult climbing; careful footwork essential
The top-out from the ice couloir onto rock can be confusing; scout the exit before committing to the ice
Rappel anchors can be marginal; test anchors before weighting them
Glacier crevasses on Price Glacier require care; a rope team is recommended
Creek crossings on approach can be difficult in high water (typically mid-June through July); consider an early start or late crossing
routes.bail: If conditions are poor or progress is slow, retreat back down the ice couloir to the bergschrund. Descend via rappel to Price Glacier and return to camp. There is no straightforward escape route from the upper rock terrain once past the couloir exit—commit to the climb only if conditions permit.
routes.turnaround: Establish a firm turnaround time by mid-morning (10 a.m. from the bergschrund) to ensure adequate daylight for the technical descent. If weather deteriorates, descend immediately; do not press on hoping for conditions to improve. The descent is more dangerous than the ascent due to rappels and loose rock.
routes.comms: Cell phone service is unreliable or absent on the route. Mount Shuksan blocks line-of-sight to Mount Baker Ski Area towers. A satellite messenger or personal locator beacon (PLB) is strongly recommended for emergencies.

routes.overview: A more challenging alternative route involving technical rock and ice climbing on the south face of Nooksack Tower. This 12-pitch route climbs primarily rock with significant ice and snow sections, offering an alpine objective for skilled climbers seeking a more serious undertaking.
routes.beta: The route begins on the right side of the south face with front-pointing up an ice/snow couloir, then exiting left onto a sloping belay ledge. Exchange ice tools for rock shoes for the rock sections. The first several pitches angle slightly left and up with a short overhanging bulge, eventually leading into a depression. Simulclimbing ascends left out of the depression to the left-center portion of the south face. The climb continues up loose, poor-quality rock toward a small tower; after a rappel, easy class 4 rock leads to the summit. Total rope length is approximately 200 feet for efficient simulclimbing sections.
routes.approach: Approach via Price Glacier and East Nooksack Glacier to base of south face. Establish camp around 5,900 feet and approach the south face base (approximately 1,500 feet higher elevation gain). Route-finding to the south face base is more complex than standard north face approaches.
routes.descent: Descend by rappel from the small tower and traverse back to glaciers, then return to camp.
routes.bestSeason: July and August only, when snow consolidation is good and rock is driest. South face receives more direct sun, potentially increasing rockfall hazard.
routes.detailedRack: Bring a full rock rack (nuts 0.5–2 inches, cams 0.5–3.5 inches), ice screws (several 65cm), pitons, multiple slings, and carabiners. The poor rock quality on the south face means protection is marginal; expect minimal and problematic placements.
routes.proNeeds: Rock protection is extremely challenging due to poor quality rock and limited crack systems. Expect to place pitons in marginal placements or rely on ice screws when in snow/ice sections. Be prepared to simulclimb sections where protection is unavailable.
proTips: Scout the route entrance on the right side of the south face before committing
The left-center simulclimbing section is critical for time; optimize your footwork to move quickly
Move with urgency in the rock sections to minimize exposure to rockfall
watchOut: Rockfall potential is higher on the south face due to sun exposure and friable rock
Poor rock quality throughout; extreme care required for protection placement and footwork
The small tower and final pitches are exposed and escape options are limited
This face is rarely climbed and may have few established anchors
routes.bail: Early retreat is critical. If protection is problematic or climbing feels unsafe, descend back down the couloir entrance before reaching mid-face where retreat becomes very difficult.
routes.turnaround: Establish a firm turnaround time by 10 a.m. from the base of the face to ensure adequate daylight for the descent.

climate.typical: South face exposed to afternoon sun and warming. Expect rapid temperature increase and potential for increased rockfall later in the day.
climate.summer: July–August optimal; south face typically has better snow consolidation by mid-summer.

blurb: Nooksack Tower (8,285 feet) is an outlier of the Mount Shuksan massif in North Cascades National Park, located 0.75 miles northeast of Shuksan's summit. Composed of friable greenschist, the peak presents one of the most challenging and objectively hazardous alpine climbing objectives in the Cascades, featuring loose rock, steep terrain, and significant crevassed glaciers. The classic Beckey-Schmidtke route ascends 800 feet of 50-degree ice in the north couloir, followed by class 4–5 scrambling on marginal-quality rock, earning first ascent on July 5, 1946.
hierarchyNote:
CRITICAL ELEVATION DISCREPANCY: The provided peak elevation of 9,050 feet is significantly incorrect. Multiple authoritative sources (SummitPost, Mountain Project, Wikipedia, Peakbagger, PeakVisor, and American Alpine Club publications) consistently document Nooksack Tower as 8,285 feet (2,525 m), representing a 765-foot error. All other identifying information (peak name, location within North Cascades National Park, coordinates 48.83102, -121.60283, parent area as Mount Shuksan massif outlier) is accurate and verified.</hierarchyNote>

routes.overview: The original north face climbing line and the primary alpine rock climb on Mount Terror's imposing north side. Mostly Class 3-4 scrambling interspersed with technical sections, meeting the long snow finger that forms an east ramp on the upper face.
routes.beta: This is the first documented route on Mount Terror's north-facing terrain, established in 1961. The route ascends the right (west) of two ribs in the north face, utilizing snow and rock sections. The crux is located near the top of a chimney midway up the face.
routes.fa: Charles Bell, Ed Cooper, Dave Hiser, and Mike Swayne (July 8-11, 1961)
routes.approach: From Goodell Creek Group Campground trailhead (elevation 600 ft), hike old road for 4.5 miles paralleling Terror Creek. Continue to Terror Creek crossing and ascend to Terror Basin. From base camp at Crescent Creek Basin (5,800 ft), continue over Otto-Himmel col and descend on snow below Twin Spires and The Rake to reach base of north face. Fastest parties will need 2-3 hours from Crescent Creek to base of route.
routes.bestSeason: August is optimal when most seasonal snow has melted from lower elevations. July also viable but buggier. September possible but concern for early season snow and reduced daylight.
routes.descent: Four very steep rappels down the east corner of the face.
routes.descentText: Rather than descending the normal route (which proved hidden from view), the first ascent team completed four very steep rappels down the east corner, a descent method favored on subsequent ascents.

routes.overview: A less popular Class 5.6 alpine rock route involving two pitches of technical climbing, a traverse across loose rock sections on the south face to a saddle, then two more pitches with Class 5 mixed rock to the summit. Characterized by poor rock quality and complex route-finding.
routes.beta: Access via a steep descent into Crescent Creek Basin on mixed snow and talus, followed by an hour-long rising traverse across the basin. Approach culminates at approximately 400 feet of wet slabby Class 3-4 climbing to reach the ridge saddle between Mount Terror and Mount Degenhardt. From saddle, climb two pitches reaching the south face traverse, cross loose terrain on the south face, then wrap up again with two additional pitches of 5th class rock to the summit.
routes.face: East Ridge with South Face Traverse
routes.approach: From Crescent Creek Basin base camp, descend to basin floor on mixed snow and talus. Traverse east across basin for approximately 1 hour to reach slabs. Climb 400 feet of wet Class 3-4 slab climbing to saddle between Terror and Degenhardt.
routes.descent: Downclimb and scramble via the West Ridge
routes.descentText: Descend via West Ridge using Class 3-4 scrambling, approximately 3-4 hours

routes.routeName: Goodell Creek Approach to West Ridge (Standard Route)
routes.overview: The standard approach and most frequently climbed route on Mount Terror. A Class 3-4 alpine scramble with one low 5th class crux pitch. This is the most accessible of Mount Terror's major routes, requiring significant mountaineering skills despite the 'easiest' designation on this remote peak.
routes.beta: Start from Goodell Creek Group Campground and follow trail for 4.5 miles on old road bed paralleling Terror Creek to approximately 2,100 feet. Cross Terror Creek (potentially challenging during high water) and ascend through mixed terrain via The Barrier (steep, loose scramble to 3,400 ft), traverse Stump Hollow, reach Chopping Block col. From here, descend and traverse on scree/talus to access the west ridge gully approach. Ascend the 400-foot gully (mostly snow with moat crossing hazards) to reach the notch, then climb technical rock to the ridge proper. Continue on Class 3-4 scrambling with running belay to summit block.
routes.fa: William Degenhardt and Herbert Strandberg (Seattle Mountaineers, 1932)
routes.approach: Goodell Creek Group Campground (elevation 600 ft) trailhead. Hike old forestry road for 4.5 miles paralleling Terror Creek to approximately 2,100 feet elevation. Route-finding crucial; faint path exists but can be lost. Cross Terror Creek on logs/debris. Navigate The Barrier (steep ascent to 3,400 ft). Traverse Stump Hollow to locate flat bivy site approximately 100 yards north of low notch. Total approach from trailhead to camp: 6.5-9.5 hours depending on water conditions and party fitness.
routes.descent: Two rappels to gully top; three additional rappels within gully with downclimbing between sections. Total descent from camp: approximately 9 hours. Approximately 4 hours additional to return to trailhead.
routes.descentText: From the summit, downclimb and establish rappel stations on the ridge. Make two rappels to reach the gully. Enter the gully and make three additional rappels with downclimbing between rappels, ascending carefully due to moat undercutting. Exit gully and descend scree/talus back to camp.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Hike from Goodell Creek trailhead to camp at Stump Hollow or Crescent Creek Basin (6.5-9.5 hours, elevation gain 6,000 ft). Day 2: Summit attempt from camp, gaining additional 1,600-2,000 feet; 5+ hours to summit, 4-5 hours descent back to camp. Day 3: Descend from camp to trailhead (6-8 hours).
routes.bail: From any point on approach, descend back to Terror Creek and follow creek downstream (if water level permits) or retrace route. If caught on exposed terrain above Chopping Block, descend to Crescent Creek Basin and traverse back. From high on route, establish belay/rappel stations as needed.
routes.turnaround: Establish firm turnaround time based on daylight available; allow minimum 3-4 hours for descent from summit block to camp. Descent in darkness is extremely hazardous on loose terrain.
routes.bestSeason: August is optimal when most seasonal snow has melted from approach. July viable but buggier; September possible but watch for early season snow and shorter daylight.

blurb: Mount Terror stands at 8,151 feet as the highest peak of the Southern Picket Range in North Cascades National Park, Washington. First climbed in 1932 by William Degenhardt and Herbert Strandberg, this remote alpine peak features steep granite faces with 5,000+ feet of vertical relief dropping to the McMillan Cirque. Its signature loose, narrow gully and exposed Class 3-5 scrambling routes demand serious mountaineering skills despite modest technical grades; the peak's character is defined by poor rock quality, significant rockfall hazard, and remote access requiring exceptional navigation across rough, trail-less terrain.

hierarchyNote: ELEVATION DISCREPANCY FOUND: Input data lists 8,494 feet, but all authoritative sources (Wikipedia, Mountain Project, SummitPost, Peakbagger, USGS, Peakery) confirm 7,492 feet (2,284 m). This is a 1,000+ foot error. Peak location and hierarchy (Alpine Lakes Wilderness, border of Snoqualmie/Wenatchee NF, ~2 miles west of Mount Daniel) verified as correct and geographically plausible.
blurb: Mount Hinman (7,492 ft) in the Alpine Lakes Wilderness features three glaciated faces and is a classic Class 2 alpine scramble in the North Cascades. The remote peak was first summited in 1928 and named in 1934 for Dr. Harry B. Hinman, founder of the Everett branch of The Mountaineers. Its Hinman Glacier (north face) and Foss Glacier (northeast face) are rapidly retreating.

routes.routeName: Standard Route (West Ridge via La Bohn)
routes.bestSeason: Late July through late September offers the easiest travel conditions with established routes and manageable snow conditions. Earlier seasons bring excessive snow on approaches; later seasons bring cold and variable weather. Snow and glaciers are present year-round.
routes.approach: From SR 2 east of Skykomish, drive Foss River Road (FR 68) 4.1 miles to East Fork Foss River trailhead (1,600 ft). Hike trail #102 for 9 miles through Necklace Valley (following an old railroad grade initially, then ascending steeply through rock gardens) to Opal Lake. From Opal Lake, scramble off-trail up an extremely steep slope next to a waterfall to reach La Bohn Lakes (established camp). Total approach is 9 miles one-way with 5,100 feet of gain.
routes.overview: The most popular and fastest route to Hinman's summit via the Necklace Valley approach, involving sustained Class 2 boulder scrambling and optional glacier travel from La Bohn Lakes.
routes.beta: From La Bohn Lakes, ascend due north from La Bohn Gap climbing rocky terrain with miles of boulder hopping but nothing more than Class 2. The route ascends the west ridge toward the summit, generally following the ridge or staying slightly left. True summit lies approximately 0.5 miles along the ridge; many parties settle for nearby high points.
routes.descent: Retrace the approach via La Bohn Lakes and Necklace Valley. Some parties descend the northeast shoulder on glacier slabs (weather/conditions dependent) to alpine tarns, then rejoin standard approach.
routes.descentText: Descend retracing the ascent route, reversing the Class 2 boulder terrain back to La Bohn Lakes, then backpack out through Necklace Valley via the trail. Descent is straightforward boulder scrambling. In good snow conditions (July), descent can be faster via snowfield traverses.

timing.recommendedStart: 1-2 days before summit day from Opal Lake camp

routes.detailedRack: Lightweight crampons (aluminum adequate), lightweight ice axe (not technical tools), helmet. Rope optional for team travel. Glacier gear if traversing existing snow patches.
routes.proNeeds: None required for Class 2 terrain, but crampons and ice axe highly recommended for glacier/snow navigation.
watchOut: Steep boulder terrain with ankle-twist hazard; use caution on loose rock.
Mosquitoes are notably severe in Necklace Valley; bring bug spray.
Stream crossings may be impassable during high snowmelt.
Route-finding above La Bohn Lakes is critical; stay on ridge or slightly left to avoid cliffs on right side.

Receding glaciers leaving exposed moats and crevasses in early season
Steep off-trail approach through vegetated terrain
Persistent snowfields high on peak year-round
Weather exposure on exposed ridge

routes.turnaround: Plan turnaround by 2pm on summit day. Do not push into afternoon thunderstorm window. Alpine terrain offers no shelter.
proTips: Start from Opal Lake camp for manageable summit day (7-8 hours total).
Bring traction: crampons or microspikes essential for scree and potential ice.
Water on approach is abundant; cache for summit day or carry hydration bladder.

routes.bestSeason: Late July through late September for optimal conditions. Early season (July) features extensive snowcover; late season (October) brings unpredictable weather.
routes.approach: Via Middle Fork Snoqualmie approach: From Dingford Creek gate on NFS Road 1030, bike ~9 miles to Dutch Miller Gap trailhead. Hike/bike Forest Road 56 along north side of Middle Fork Snoqualmie River (~13 miles) to Pedro Camp (milepost 13), continue through heather meadows to Dutch Miller Gap (5,000 ft). From gap, ascend off-trail northeast toward Hinman. Total approach 16 miles one-way with 5,300 feet of gain.
routes.overview: Eastern approach ascending from Dutch Miller Gap on the Middle Fork Snoqualmie side, featuring sustained Class 2 scrambling on exposed ridgelines with views into chain lakes basin.
routes.beta: Starting at 6,000 feet on the east side, climb a short rock scramble to a low-angle ridge which is followed all the way up to the summit. Ridge-line route involves boulder hopping similar to west ridge approach. Route can be snow-covered early season (July) or rock-scramble in August-September. If climbing in doubt, stay on ridge or slightly left to avoid cliffs on climber's right.
routes.descent: Descend via northeast shoulder on glacier slabs (1,500 feet) to alpine tarns, then rejoin approach route via Dutch Miller Gap. Alternatively retrace ascent ridge.
routes.descentText: Descent typically follows the ascent ridge in reverse, which is straightforward Class 2 scrambling. In snow conditions, can descend northeast shoulder onto remaining glacier slabs for rapid descent, but requires assessment of crevasse danger and ice conditions.

timing.recommendedStart: 1-2 days before summit from Dutch Miller Gap area camp

routes.detailedRack: Ice axe and crampons recommended for glacier/snow sections. Helmet for rockfall protection. Minimal rock protection needed given Class 2 terrain.
watchOut: Rock scramble terrain with ankle-twist hazard on loose boulder fields.
Right side of ridge is cliffy; stay on ridge or slightly left.
Northeast shoulder descent requires crevasse and ice assessment.
Exposure on ridge traverse with significant drops both sides.

routes.bestSeason: Late August through September when Foss Glacier is consolidated enough for travel. July can have moat hazards; October brings unpredictable storms.
routes.approach: Via Necklace Valley to La Bohn Lakes (see standard route), then traverse northwest onto Foss Glacier (northeast face of Hinman) and ascend glacier to reach the west ridge summit approach. Total distance approximately 22 miles with 5,700 feet of elevation gain.
routes.overview: Glacier-based approach ascending the west face via Foss Glacier traverse and snowfield climbing from La Bohn Lakes, offering an alternative glacier travel route to the summit.
routes.beta: From La Bohn Lakes, traverse northwest onto Foss Glacier. Navigate glacier terrain with appropriate crevasse precautions. Ascend snow and glacier to reach the west ridge near summit. Route avoids the boulder scrambling of the standard ridge route but requires solid glacier travel skills and snow/ice assessment.
routes.descent: Descend via same glacier route or traverse to standard ridge descent. In good snow conditions, can bootski down Foss Glacier for rapid descent.
routes.descentText: Retreat via ascent route on Foss Glacier, or traverse to standard west ridge for descent. In perfect snow conditions (typically early August after consolidation), parties have descended 2,000 feet in 40 minutes via bootski descent from summit to base of Pea Soup waterfalls. However, this requires excellent snow conditions and is dependent on glacier stability.

timing.recommendedStart: 2-3 days before summit from La Bohn Lakes

routes.detailedRack: Crampons, ice axe, rope for team travel, harness, carabiners. Crevasse rescue kit essential.
routes.proNeeds: Glacier travel and crevasse rescue experience required. Not suitable for inexperienced glacier travelers.

Early season (July) moats are dangerous and impassable.
Glacier surface changes annually; previous years' route may not exist.

Overexposure to afternoon sun can cause dangerous slough on steep glacier.

routes.bestSeason: Late July through September for established conditions. Early season has excessive snow on trail; late season has unpredictable weather. This approach is most reliable for backcountry access.
routes.approach: From East Fork Foss River trailhead (1,600 ft), hike trail #102 through Necklace Valley. Trail begins gently on old railroad grade for 1.5 miles through second-growth timber. At mile 5, terrain rises steeply through rock gardens and alpine valleys. First Necklace Valley lake reached at mile 7.7; continue to far end of Opal Lake at mile 9 (approximately 6,700 feet elevation gain at this point). From Opal Lake, ascend extremely steep, vegetated slope with waterfall to reach La Bohn Lakes (6,200 feet). Total backcountry distance: approximately 18 miles round trip including off-trail sections.
routes.overview: The northern approach via established Necklace Valley trail, offering the shortest and most direct approach for backcountry access from SR 2 near Skykomish. Ideal for parties not requiring technical climbing.
routes.beta: This is essentially the approach component for the standard route; it is included separately as a standalone backcountry objective. The Necklace Valley trail itself is well-maintained and frequently traveled, making it one of the most reliable approaches to high alpine lakes in the region. Parties may turn around at Opal Lake or continue to La Bohn Lakes for the summit push.
routes.descent: Retrace trail from Opal Lake back down through Necklace Valley to trailhead.
routes.descentText: Descent follows the established Necklace Valley trail in reverse. Early portions are steep and rooty, requiring careful footing. Total descent to trailhead is approximately 9 miles and 5,100 feet of elevation loss.

timing.recommendedStart: Dawn departure for full approach by evening camp

routes.detailedRack: Minimal gear required for trail hiking. Trekking poles recommended for steep descent. Crampons/ice axe if continuing to La Bohn Lakes.

Trail can be muddy and slippery after rain.

routes.overview: The standard and most popular route on Bonanza Peak, following Mary Green Glacier to reach the summit via Class 4 rock scrambling. This alpine glacier route combines moderate snow/ice travel with exposed scrambling on loose orthogneiss.
routes.beta: From Holden Lake (5,278 ft), scramble up slabs to the northern edge of Mary Green Glacier. Rope up and ascend the glacier bearing west along its north edge, then traverse southwest along the base of the northeast ridge until reaching the glacier's upper corner. Climb the steep upper snow finger to climber's right until its highest point, then exit left onto rock. Climb Class 3-4 rock right of a gully for several rope lengths, traverse left into another gully and scramble to a prominent notch in the upper northeast ridge just north of the summit (Class 4). Scramble along the loose ridge crest (Class 4) past large blocks to the summit horn.
routes.approach: From Holden Village/Holden Ballpark Campground (3,300 ft), take the Railroad Creek Trail approximately 1 mile to its junction with the Holden Lake Trail (3,600 ft). Turn right and hike 4 miles to Holden Lake (5,278 ft). Continue north-northwest from the head of the lake to Holden Pass (6,400 ft) for high camp, or continue directly to glacier approaches. Approach is approximately 1 mile from Holden Lake trailhead area. Total approach distance from Holden Campground is approximately 11-13 miles round trip.
routes.descent: Downclimb and rappel the route. Parties typically make three to four rappels on the upper mountain and east face waterfall slabs, using both 50m and 60m ropes. Retrace the route back to the glacier, then traverse the glacier back to Holden Lake and descend the approach trail.
routes.descentText: The descent mirrors the ascent in reverse. From the summit, downclimb or rappel the loose ridge crest and notch section (Class 4). Traverse left into the gully system and downclimb/rappel (Class 3-4 rock). With adequate rope, parties make two to four rappels to regain the glacier and the slabs below. Traverse the Mary Green Glacier back to its lower edge, descending the approach slabs to Holden Lake.
routes.fa: Curtis I. James, Barrie James, Joe Leuthold (Mazamas), 1937
routes.rappels: 3-4 rappels, up to 200 feet each; 50-60m ropes recommended
routes.bestSeason: Early to mid July offers optimal conditions when the bergschrund can still be easily crossed but most snow is away from the rock scrambling route. Most successful teams climb between June and October, with July the most reliable month. Winter and early spring ascents are typically unfavorable due to corniced ridges and avalanche danger.
knownHazards: Rock quality is poor—loose, metamorphic orthogneiss with frequent rockfall risk, especially when rain loosens material or temperatures warm the peak.
Glacier crevasses are a serious hazard; rope teams required.
The bergschrund crossing can be dangerous in early/late season; optimize timing for reliable snow bridges.
The Class 4 scrambling is exposed and loose, with significant rockfall hazard from above; wear a helmet and move efficiently.
Weather can deteriorate rapidly; inclement weather is an extreme hazard on this mountain.
watchOut: Helmet mandatory due to frequent rockfall.
Careful route-finding required to keep climbing in Class 3 terrain; wandering into steeper terrain increases exposure.
Check bergschrund crossability early in approach; retreat if conditions unsafe.
Black flies and mosquitoes are severe during descent in mid-summer.
Loose rock, especially in gully sections; minimize party exposure by moving one at a time on rock.
proTips: Camp at Holden Pass (6,400 ft) rather than Holden Lake to save 1,000 vertical feet on summit day.
Early-to-mid July is the sweet spot for climbing—bergschrund crossable, most rock free of snow, and weather generally stable.
Navigate to climber's right of the Mary Green Glacier on approach to stay on easier terrain.
Bring both 50m and 60m ropes for descent; four rappels with 50-60m ropes is most efficient.
gear: Full alpine rack (nuts, cams sizes 0-3, slings)
routes.detailedRack: Alpine rack with a mix of cams (sizes 0-2.5), nuts (sizes 1-8), and several 120cm dyneema slings. For glacier travel: crampons, ice axe, harness, and glacier protection (pickets or snow anchors). Bring two pickets for snow anchors on the glacier.
routes.proNeeds: Rock protection can be sparse; much of the climbing is on loose, untrustworthy terrain. Anchor building relies on slings around features and natural anchors (blocks, gully anchors). Two well-protected rappel stations each for descent security.
whatToBring: Tent and sleeping bag rated for alpine conditions
Climbing shoes or approach shoes with good edge
Warm hat, sun hat, and gloves

timing.recommendedStart: Early morning from high camp (4-5am) to maximize daylight and minimize exposure.

routes.itinerary: Day 1: Travel to Holden Campground via Lake Chelan ferry and bus (full day logistics, approximately 8-10 hours from Seattle). Hike to Holden Lake or camp at Holden Pass (3-5 hours hiking). Day 2: Summit day from high camp: 4-5 hours ascent, 1-2 hours summit, 4-5 hours descent. Return to Holden Lake camp. Day 3: Descend to Holden Campground and reverse ferry/bus logistics to reach Seattle.
routes.bail: From the glacier, retreat to Holden Lake and descend via the approach trail. If on the rock above the glacier and conditions worsen, descend to the glacier if possible. If above the glacier and weather is severe, bivy and wait for conditions, or call for helicopter rescue. Chelan County Mountain Rescue and Chelan County Sheriff's Air Support can conduct high-altitude rescues.
routes.turnaround: Turn around if you have not reached the base of the final 500-foot rock section by mid-morning, or if the upper mountain is experiencing rockfall or storm conditions.

access.fees: Northwest Forest Pass ($30 annual or $5 day-use parking fee) required at Holden Campground. Ferry and shuttle bus to Holden Village/Chelan are separate and add significant logistics costs.

access.closures: Holden Village is closed to vehicles and hikers through summer 2026 due to storm damage. Access via Lucerne and bus service may resume; check with Holden Village or Chelan Boat Company for current status.

road.driveNote: Drive to Chelan, Washington (approximately 3 hours from Seattle). Take ferry from Chelan to Lucerne (4 hours), then board shuttle bus to Holden Village (13 miles, 1-2 hours). No vehicle access beyond Lucerne. This requires full-day logistics from Seattle.

climate.typical: Alpine Cascades climate; cool, wet, and changeable. July average high 45-50°F at summit (9,511 ft), lows near freezing. August slightly warmer.
climate.spring: May-June: Heavy snow at upper elevations; crevasse danger higher due to thin snow bridges; bergschrund often difficult to cross.
climate.summer: June-August: Most stable season. July offers best conditions with manageable snow and low avalanche danger. August warming can increase rockfall and crevasse danger.
climate.fall: September: Conditions deteriorate; snow returns to high elevations; shorter daylight limits safe climbing windows.
climate.winter: October-April: Extreme avalanche danger, corniced summit ridge, severe weather; not recommended for ascent.

routes.comms: Cell service is unreliable or absent in the Glacier Peak Wilderness area. Satellite communicators (Garmin InReach, PLBs) are strongly recommended for emergencies. Ferry crossing at Lake Chelan may have limited coverage.

emergency.notes: Rescue from Bonanza Peak is extremely challenging; helicopter evacuation is possible but weather-dependent. Chelan County Mountain Rescue and Chelan County Sheriff's Air Support conduct backcountry rescues. Notify Holden Village of trip plans before departure.

routes.overview: Alpine rock climb on the prominent northeast buttress leading to the main summit, featuring 5.7-5.8 climbing on solid but lichen-covered rock with wild exposure. Often climbed as the first part of the Three-Summit Traverse.
routes.beta: Access via Mary Green Glacier approach to the right (climber's right of the glacier), which provides easy access to the base of the Northeast Buttress at 8,350 feet. The buttress features mostly solid lichen-covered rock climbed on or around the crest in the 5.7 range. Navigation is straightforward—follow the ridge crest with wild exposure throughout. The route steepens and narrows immediately, maintaining technical terrain.
routes.approach: From Holden Lake, traverse to approach terrain right of Mary Green Glacier (easier terrain than direct glacier line). Scramble gain approximately 1,000 vertical feet to reach the base of the Northeast Buttress at 8,350 feet from Holden Lake (approximately 3 hours from lake). Can be combined with higher camp at Holden Pass for smaller approach on summit day.
routes.descent: From the main summit, descend south to the ridge between main and west summits, or retrace the Northeast Buttress if not continuing the traverse. If doing the Three-Summit Traverse, descend to Isella Glacier and traverse to Holden Ridge, then cross ridge and descend Mary Green Glacier to Holden Lake.
routes.descentText: Downclimb the ridge crest (5.7 terrain, Class 4 moves). Follow the ridge line down, staying on or just left of the crest. Return to the base of the buttress via scrambling and Class 3-4 terrain. If returning to Mary Green Glacier, traverse right/west back to glacier terrain and descend glacier as for standard route.
routes.fa: First traversed August 21-22, 2004 (Three-Summit Traverse): Kurt Buchwald, Peter Avolio, Martin Volken (AAC)
routes.bestSeason: Mid-July to early September for stable rock and snow conditions. Earlier in the season, snow and ice can coat the rock; later season increases rockfall hazard and complexity.
knownHazards: Rock quality is poor; orthogneiss is loose and lichen-covered, making holds unreliable.
Exposure is significant and continuous—steep walls with runout potential if protection fails.
Lichen-covered stone is slippery when wet; weather deterioration is dangerous on this exposed ridge.
watchOut: Helmet mandatory.
Sharp exposure throughout—secure all movement; place protection frequently.
Lichen reduces friction; be extra cautious on wet rock.
The ridge is narrow; avoid exposure by staying on the crest.
proTips: Approach terrain to climber's right of Mary Green Glacier to avoid glacier hazards.
Climb efficiently to minimize time on exposed ridge; the sooner you move through, the safer.
Consider the Three-Summit Traverse if doing more technical climbing; combined approach is more efficient.
routes.detailedRack: Climbing rack with cams (sizes 0-2.5) and nuts (sizes 1-8), plus 4-6 slings (120cm dyneema). Anchors can be built with slings around features; direct rock anchors are often inadequate due to loose rock. Use multi-point anchors wherever possible.
routes.proNeeds: Limited protection opportunities on some pitches; lichen and loose rock make gear placement unreliable. Look for bomber natural features (stacked blocks, locked stones in the buttress) for anchors. Many pitches require #2 to #2.5 cams or medium nuts.

timing.recommendedStart: Early morning (4-5am) from high camp or after reaching glacier approach by late morning.

routes.itinerary: Day 1: Travel to Holden and hike to high camp (Holden Pass or similar). Day 2: Hike to glacier approach terrain and reach the Northeast Buttress base. Climb buttress to main summit (6-7 hours round trip). Continue to west or southwest summit if doing the traverse. Return to camp. Day 3: Descend to trailhead and reverse ferry/bus logistics.
routes.bail: Early in the route, can descend back to Mary Green Glacier area and retrace to approach. High on the buttress, reversing is exposed; continue to summit and descend via standard route. In storm, bivy on the ridge if necessary or signal for helicopter rescue.
routes.turnaround: Turn around if you have not reached the summit or key waypoints by mid-day to ensure you can complete the descent in daylight.

climate.spring: Heavy snow; route may be snow/ice covered.
climate.summer: July-August: Optimal; clear rock and stable conditions.

routes.overview: Class 3-4 scramble on the east face above Mary Green Glacier, featuring a steep gully system with slab climbing. Less trafficked than the standard Mary Green Glacier route but similar commitment and technical level.
routes.beta: From Mary Green Glacier approach, traverse right (climber's right) on ledges toward small waterfalls and slabs below the glacier (Class 3 scrambling). Continue approximately 100 feet to find mostly dry, climbable slabs leading to the bottom of the glacier or to the base of the east face gully system. Start up a short spur to an obvious gully on the left. Climb mostly Class 3 with a slabby dihedral section (Class 4) for several hundred feet. Traverse left to reach the east ridge about 100 vertical feet below the summit. Finish on or left of the ridge crest (Class 3-4) to the summit horn.
routes.approach: From Holden Lake, hike to Mary Green Glacier terrain (approximately 6-7 miles). Traverse right toward slabs and waterfalls on the east side of the glacier base. This approach route combines glacier access with rock terrain and is approximately 1-2 hours of scrambling to reach the base of the east face gully.
routes.descent: Downclimb and/or rappel the route. Parties may choose to descend via the Mary Green Glacier standard route if that is more convenient. East face descent can involve several rappels on the slabby terrain; bring both ropes.
routes.descentText: Downclimb the east ridge crest (Class 3-4) toward the notch. Descend the gully system (Class 3-4 with potential for one or two rappels). Traverse back to the Mary Green Glacier slabs. Either continue down to the glacier via slabs or make a short rappel to reach the glacier/moraine area. Retrace to Holden Lake via the glacier.
routes.bestSeason: Early to mid-July for optimal conditions. June may have snow on slabs; late season increases rockfall hazard.
knownHazards: The gully is extremely dangerous with rockfall from parties above; helmets mandatory and efficient movement essential.
Wet slabs near waterfalls can be slippery; dry slab climbing is more reliable.
Loose orthogneiss rock can shift underfoot; test all holds before committing weight.
Late summer rockfall increases significantly due to warming and ice melt.
watchOut: Helmet absolutely mandatory—rockfall hazard is high in the gully.
Avoid climbing if other parties are above on the east face.
Waterfalls indicate significant runoff; plan timing to minimize wet rock exposure.
Slabby terrain on dihedral section requires good technique; falls can be serious.
proTips: This route offers an alternative to the more crowded Mary Green Glacier line; use it if other parties occupy the standard route.
Start early to avoid afternoon sun warming and rockfall increase.
The slabby dihedral section is the crux; take time to find good slab technique and protection.
gear: Alpine rock rack (lightweight selection: cams 0.5-2, nuts 1-5)
routes.detailedRack: Lightweight alpine rack with medium cams (#1-2) and nuts (#2-6), plus 3-4 slings. Most climbing is unroped scrambling; bring gear for possible rappel anchors.
routes.proNeeds: Limited protection opportunities; mostly unroped scrambling. If roping up for the slabby dihedral section, place gear in cracks and behind loose blocks. Natural anchors (chokestones, trees) are often available.
whatToBring: Climbing shoes or approach boots with edge

timing.recommendedStart: Early morning from high camp (5am or earlier).

routes.itinerary: Day 1: Travel to Holden and hike to high camp. Day 2: Approach to east face slabs, climb to summit (8-9 hours round trip from camp). Return to camp. Day 3: Descend to trailhead and reverse logistics.
routes.bail: Early in the route, retreat to Mary Green Glacier approach. Once on the slabby dihedral, downclimbing is difficult; commit to the route or rappel if conditions worsen.
routes.turnaround: Turn around if you have not reached the east ridge by mid-morning.

routes.overview: An alternative approach to Bonanza Peak via the west face and gully systems, possibly including access to the southwest summit area. Less documented than the Mary Green Glacier route; technical details vary based on specific line chosen.
routes.beta: Access via west-facing gully systems above the Isella Glacier or via alternate approaches from the Holden area. The west face can be accessed by traversing from the upper Mary Green Glacier or by approaching via the southwest col between Bonanza and North Star Peak. Various gully systems lead up the west face; most feature Class 3-4 scrambling interspersed with potential climbing sections. Ascent typically combines glacier travel, gully scrambling, and exposed ridge or face climbing to reach the main summit or one of the subsidiary summits.
routes.approach: From Holden Lake, hike to Mary Green Glacier terrain. Traverse left/west across the upper glacier or traverse around the west side of Bonanza Peak via the Isella Glacier and approach the west face gully systems. Alternatively, approach from the upper Holden area via the col between North Star and Bonanza Peak. Approach distance is variable depending on specific line chosen; typically 2-4 additional hours beyond Holden Lake.
routes.descent: Descend via the Isella Glacier and traverse back to Holden Lake, or retrace the ascent route if remaining on west face terrain. Traverse to Holden Ridge and descend to Mary Green Glacier, then retrace to Holden Lake.
routes.descentText: Return via the route of ascent, or traverse via Isella Glacier and Holden Ridge. The descent is exposed and complex; careful route-finding is essential to avoid unintended exposure.
routes.bestSeason: Mid-July to early September when snow is clear from gullies and approach terrain. Earlier in season, snow and avalanche danger are higher.
knownHazards: Gully systems funnel rockfall; helmet mandatory.
Route-finding is complex and requires careful navigation; parties can become lost or exposed.
The west face is less frequented and may be less protected by peers; choose weather windows carefully.
In early season, avalanche danger in gully approach terrain is significant.
watchOut: Helmet mandatory.
Carry map and compass; GPS helpful for navigation in complex terrain.
Avoid gullies during afternoon rockfall periods.
Check conditions before committing to approach; retreat if unsafe.
proTips: Scout alternative descent routes before beginning ascent.
Gully climbing is often faster in the morning before rock warms and becomes loose.
The west face offers solitude compared to the Mary Green Glacier route; plan accordingly for longer approaches and complex route-finding.
routes.detailedRack: Moderate alpine rack with a mix of cams and nuts; assume limited protection opportunities on gully terrain. Build anchors from natural features or multi-point slings.
routes.proNeeds: Protection is sparse and unreliable; assume mostly unroped scrambling with occasional short protected sections.

timing.recommendedStart: Very early (4am) from high camp or via an additional bivy on approach.

routes.itinerary: Day 1: Travel to Holden and hike to high camp. Day 2: Approach to west face base via glacier and gully systems (full day). Day 3: Summit attempt, climb to summit and descend (8-10 hours). Day 4: Return to trailhead and reverse logistics (for most parties; aggressive teams might summit and descend to camp in one long day).
routes.bail: Retreat to most recent camp or secure location if conditions worsen. Gully systems may be difficult to reverse; ensure you can downclimb safely or be prepared for bivy.
routes.turnaround: Turn around if you have not made significant progress by late morning; descent is safer in daylight.

blurb:
Bonanza Peak is the highest non-volcanic peak in Washington State at 9,511 feet, located in the Glacier Peak Wilderness of the North Cascades. Flanked by three major glaciers (Mary Green, Company, and Isella), the peak presents one of the most technically demanding county highpoints in the lower 48 states, requiring a diverse skill set combining glacier travel, exposed rock scrambling (Class 4), and alpine mountaineering. First ascended in 1937 by the Mazamas, the peak draws experienced climbers seeking an intermediate alpine alpine experience with significant logistical challenge.</blurb>

Named peak is confirmed as highest non-volcanic peak in Washington and is part of the massif with multiple sub-summits. Three named glaciers verified: Mary Green Glacier (E), Company Glacier (N), Isella Glacier (S).
